ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general cabbage entry; no dedicated boiled-cabbage term exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cabbages and other brassica vegetables, cooked
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked brassica vegetables including cabbage; used as closest defensible code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for boiled cabbage as a food item; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled cabbage is approximately 150 g (about 1 cup cooked), providing roughly 35 kcal and 8.3 g of carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Cabbage, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11110)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo values used as primary source (confidence 0.95, reviewed). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for boiled cabbage (NDB 11110) used as secondary cross-reference to confirm macro and micronutrient plausibility. Values from HPB are retained as primary; USDA confirms alignment. Saturated fat sourced from USDA SR Legacy (0.03 g/100 g); monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values also from USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for boiled cabbage exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Cabbage is a non-starchy leafy vegetable with very low carbohydrate content (5.5 g/100 g). A GI of approximately 10 is estimated by analogy with other boiled non-starchy brassica vegetables (e.g., broccoli GI ~10, cauliflower GI ~15) as documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). GL per 150 g serving = (10 × 5.51 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.83, rounded to 0.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (8 mg/100 g) and high potassium (196 mg/100 g) supporting favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Boiled cabbage is highly suitable for hypertension management; its sodium content of 8 mg/100 g and potassium of 196 mg/100 g align with DASH dietary principles, and regular consumption may contribute to blood pressure reduction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.5 g/100 g), low sugar (2.8 g/100 g), and low estimated GI (~10) with moderate dietary fibre (1.9 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled cabbage has a very low glycaemic index (~10) and low glycaemic load per serving (~0.8), making it an excellent choice for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its fibre content further slows glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.03 g/100 g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and presence of dietary fibre and potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled cabbage is cardioprotective due to its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and meaningful potassium and fibre content, all of which support healthy lipid profiles and vascular function.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cruciferous vegetable with sulforaphane and vitamin C (~28 mg/100 g) with documented anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled cabbage is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; its sulforaphane content and vitamin C (~28 mg/100 g) provide anti-inflammatory benefits, and its negligible saturated fat does not promote systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled cabbage is a minimally processed vegetable dish prepared by submerging fresh cabbage leaves in boiling water until tender. The result is pale green, soft leaves with a mild, slightly sweet flavour and a tender yet slightly firm core. It is a globally consumed preparation with no specific regional origin, commonly served as a side dish, salad base, or light snack across Asian, European, and Western cuisines. Nutritionally, boiled cabbage is very low in calories (23 kcal/100 g), low in carbohydrates (5.5 g/100 g), and contains negligible fat (0.06 g/100 g) and very low sodium (8 mg/100 g). Its potassium content (196 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (1.9 g/100 g) make it suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The estimated glycaemic index of approximately 10 and glycaemic load of 0.8 per 150 g serving make it an excellent choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. As a cruciferous vegetable, cabbage contains sulforaphane and vitamin C (~28 mg/100 g), which have documented anti-inflammatory properties relevant to rheumatoid arthritis. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements.
common_user_queries: Is boiled cabbage good for diabetes?
How many calories in boiled cabbage?
Can I eat boiled cabbage if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of boiled cabbage?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for boiled cabbage (NDB 11110) confirms macro and micronutrient alignment. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, and polyunsaturated fat supplemented from USDA SR Legacy. GI estimated by analogy with peer-reviewed brassica GI data from Atkinson et al. 2008.
notes: 1. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — boiled cabbage contains no trans fats by nature (naturally zero for a plain boiled vegetable, confirmed by USDA SR Legacy). 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero — plant-based food contains no cholesterol. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo primary record for this entry; while USDA SR Legacy contains some of these values, the HPB record is the canonical source for this food ID and detailed micronutrient disaggregation was not included in the reviewed HPB record. Vitamin C (~28 mg/100 g) is included based on USDA SR Legacy cross-reference and is consistent with published literature for boiled cabbage. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (~10) based on peer-reviewed brassica vegetable GI data (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for boiled cabbage was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact ontology code for boiled cabbage exists in either system as of 2026-04-16.
